title: Violating the Dead and Oil Money
body:
Modern Chinese medical research and education, on the other hand, was an American import, paid for in Standard Oil money. China was a deliberate choice, reflecting the Rockefeller family’s longstanding ties to American Baptist missionary networks already running medical work there and the Foundation’s broader chartered mission to promote “the well-being of mankind throughout the world.”[[cite:rockarch-foundation-history]] 

The Rockefeller Foundation’s China Medical Board had been established in 1914 with a $12 million endowment, modeled on the Johns Hopkins approach that had recently reformed American medical education--research-university affiliation, full-time clinical faculty, and two years of lab-based basic-science training before clinical work, a stricter standard than the missionary teaching hospitals and apprenticeship traditions then common in China.[[cite:rockarch-pumc-history]] Its adviser Simon Flexner called the project that became PUMC “the Johns Hopkins of China.”[[cite:rockfdn-pumc-flexner-quote]] In 1915, the Board purchased the Union Medical College from the London Missionary Society and rebuilt it on the site of a former royal palace in Beijing. 

Formally established in 1917, Peking Union Medical College was conceived as nothing less than “a medical school and hospital comparable with that of the leading institutions of Western civilization.”[[cite:rockarch-pumc-history]] Its small graduating classes, capped at thirty to fifty students, produced an outsized share of the country’s first generation of Western-trained medical scientists. The Harvard-trained biochemist Hsien Wu (Wu Xian) became the first Chinese to head a PUMC department, organizing its biochemistry section in 1924, and is generally credited as the founder of Chinese biochemistry as a research discipline.[[cite:zheng-hsien-wu-2012]][[cite:pmc-cams-pumc-2020]]

The Republican-era model didn’t survive the war or the political transition that followed it. Japanese forces seized PUMC in January 1942, shortly after Pearl Harbor, and shut it down for the duration of the war; medical research elsewhere scattered as scientists fled inland with whatever equipment they could carry. In January 1951, the new People’s Republic nationalized the institution, ending the era of American-funded Chinese medical education.[[cite:pumc-wikipedia]]

title: Great Leaps Forward
body:
Mao did not trust science, but he trusted the Russians. When the People's Republic was founded in 1949, the new [[entity:cas|Chinese Academy of Sciences]], was modeled on its Soviet counterpart, inheriting their biology alongside their institutional scaffolding. The most damaging import was the theories of Trofim Lysenko, who had won Stalin’s favor in the 1930s by claiming that acquired traits could be inherited[[note:aka Lamarckism]] and that Mendelian genetics was a bourgeois fraud. 

Alternative genetic theories were permitted only after Khrushchev’s “Secret Speech” of February 1956, a closed-door denunciation of Stalin at the 20th Party Congress in Moscow. Chinese geneticists led by Tan Jiazhen, a Caltech-trained Drosophila researcher, used the Qingdao Genetics Symposium that same year to argue publicly for Mendelian inheritance--the first time anyone had done so since the founding of the People’s Republic. 

However, Lysenkoism also extended, far more fatally, into the Chinese agricultural system. In 1958, Mao issued the “Eight-Point Charter of Agriculture,” directly influenced by his doctrines of close-planting and deep ploughing. Such practices contributed to the catastrophic crop failures of the Great Leap Forward and, by extension, the deadliest famine in human history. Extimates range from 15 to 45 million deaths.[[cite:cas-history-soviet-model]][[cite:lysenkoism-wikipedia]][[cite:alphahistory-lysenko-china]]

Miraculously, two major scientific projects still emerged from this period. The first was the total chemical synthesis of bovine insulin, which was first fully sequenced by Frederick Sanger in 1955. It was the first full protein sequence ever determined, and protein synthesis was the new scientific frontier. Communist China saw it as the perfect endeavor for demonstrating scientific superiority over the West. Proposed at a 1958 symposium under Wang Yinglai, the project divided more than two hundred synthetic steps among three institutions. 

On 17 September 1965, they observed crystallization of the first artificially synthesized, fully bioactive protein in human history. The accomplishment was nominated for the Nobel multiple times, but Communist policy only recognized collective effort, and the Chinese Academy of Sciences refused to name the contributing scientists. With the onset of the Cultural Revolution, Wang was forced to spend the next decade under house arrest within his own institute, attending endless struggle sessions on Mao Zedong Thought.[[cite:bcas-insulin-2024]][[cite:synthetic-insulin-wikipedia]]

The second was artemisinin. On 23 May 1967, the People’s Liberation Army launched Project 523, a top-secret military effort to find a treatment for the chloroquine-resistant malaria killing Chinese and Vietnamese troops in the jungles of Southeast Asia. The military framing put the project under PLA protection, exempting it from the Cultural Revolution’s wider attack on academic science. 

Tu Youyou, a 38-year-old researcher at the [[entity:cacms|Academy of Traditional Chinese Medicine]] trained in both modern pharmacy and classical Chinese pharmacology, was appointed to lead the herbal-medicine team in January 1969. Her group screened candidates from classical Chinese texts and folk medicine. Qinghao (青蒿, Artemisia annua, “sweet wormwood”), prescribed in Chinese medicine for intermittent fevers for some 1,500 years, showed real but inconsistent activity against malaria parasites. The unlock came from a 4th-century manuscript, Ge Hong’s Zhouhou Beijifang, which prescribed soaking qinghao in cold water rather than boiling it. Tu inferred that the active compound was heat-labile and that her standard ethanol-reflux extraction was destroying it. She switched to diethyl ether, which boils at 35°C, and the new extract produced complete inhibition in mouse and monkey models. 

There were no functioning clinical trial regulations. Tu and two colleagues dosed themselves as healthy volunteers to check for toxicity before deploying the medicine to infected patients in Hainan. The active compound, qinghaosu (青蒿素), earned Tu the Lasker Award in 2011 and the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 2015.[[cite:lasker-artemisinin-2011]][[cite:tu-youyou-wikipedia]]

Such successes were exceptions in a half-century of state-led attacks on Chinese science. The Cultural Revolution (1966–1976) closed universities to new undergraduates between 1966 and 1969 and to graduate students through 1977; the academy’s budget was slashed to less than one-sixth of its 1965 level by 1967; records document 229 scientists killed or driven to suicide.[[cite:cas-at-70-nature]][[cite:uchicago-cultural-revolution-college]]

title: Rebuilding Science After Mao
body:
In March 1978, with Deng Xiaoping consolidating power, the National Science Conference declared the rehabilitation of science and technology a strategic priority.[[cite:national-science-conference-1978]]

The National Natural Science Foundation was established in February 1986 as the financial backing for China’s peer review system for research. Weeks later, four senior physicists, alarmed by Reagan’s Strategic Defense Initiative announced two years earlier, wrote to Deng arguing that “high-tech development is a matter of international competition for national power, and China cannot stay aloof”; he approved their proposal within two days. The resulting [[reform:863-program|863 Program]] named biotechnology one of seven national priorities.[[cite:nsfc-wikipedia]][[cite:863-program-wikipedia]][[cite:chinadaily-863-program]] The Torch Program followed in 1988 to industrialize the output, and a strategic basic-research program was added in 1997.[[cite:torch-program-china]][[cite:973-program-wikipedia]]

From the early 1990s, with the Tiananmen-era brain drain still bleeding talent abroad, the state began offering returnees, also known as haigui (“sea turtles”), labs, budgets, and faculty positions to come home. 

Domestic firms began to emerge in parallel. Major General Lou Dan founded [[entity:3sbio|Shenyang Sunshine Pharmaceutical]] in 1993 to make recombinant biologics affordable, launching the rhEPO (recombinant human erythropoietin) product [[entity:3sbio|EPIAO]] in 1998 as China’s first marketed erythropoietin;[[cite:3sbio-ir-3sbio-corporate-governance]] in 1995, former chemistry teacher Zhong Huijuan started [[entity:hansoh|Hansoh Pharmaceutical]] in Lianyungang on a generics base, a firm later tied through marriage to [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]]’s Sun Piaoyang.[[cite:scmp-zhong-huijuan-hansoh-ipo]][[cite:fiercepharma-hansoh-hengrui-family]] In 1999, the [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] was founded to claim China’s 1% share of the Human Genome Project.[[cite:innovation-china-haigui-1994]]

title: Human Genome Project, Chinese CROs, and Science Parks
body:
On 9 September 1999 at “nine seconds past the ninth minute of the ninth hour,” Yang Huanming, Wang Jian, Yu Jun, and Liu Siqi formally established the [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] as a non-governmental institute under the [[entity:cas|Chinese Academy of Sciences]]. The country’s “1% Project” (sequencing roughly thirty megabases on the short arm of human chromosome 3) was modest in scope but gave China a seat at the international Human Genome Project consortium table.[[cite:bgi-history-genomics-cn]][[cite:chgc-hgp-china-protein-cell]]

Eighteen months earlier, a Chinese-born and Columbia-trained scientist Ge Li who'd been a founding scientist at a company called Pharmacopeia, a combinatorial chemistry company based in New Jersey, returned to China. He saw the potential of scaling the technology with China's highly skilled and cheap scientist workforce and set up [[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi PharmaTech]] in Shanghai’s Waigaoqiao Free Trade Zone. Offering simple combinatorial chemistry for Western pharma at a fraction of American labor costs, Ge Li inadvertently created China's first domestic CRO. As Ge liked to say, an entrepreneur could now begin a drug company “with a piece of paper, a pencil, and a credit card.”[[cite:ge-li-wikipedia]][[cite:cen-wuxi-li-words-2016]]

Shanghai’s Zhangjiang High-Tech Park, established as a national high-tech zone in 1992, started welcoming international pharmaceutical companies like [[entity:roche|Roche]], [[entity:astrazeneca|AstraZeneca]], [[entity:novartis|Novartis]], and [[entity:gsk|GSK]] by the late 2000s, alongside a growing roster of domestic contract-research firms. State tax breaks pulled in the multinationals, and the scientists they trained then cycled out into local startups. Suzhou’s BioBAY opened in June 2007 as a startup-focused complement.[[cite:biobay-suzhou-baike]] The [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] moved to Hangzhou in 2001 in exchange for municipal funding, then to Shenzhen in 2007,[[cite:ckgsb-bgi-gene-dreams]] where Yang Huanming was fond of remarking that “the mountains are high and the emperor is far away.”

title: BeiGene and Fast-Following Keytruda
body:
By 2010, more than a hundred Illumina HiSeq sequencers, bought on a ten-billion-yuan China Development Bank loan, had made the [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]] the world’s largest single producer of genomic data, more than Harvard or the United States National Institutes of Health.[[cite:illumina-bgi-128-hiseq]] 

In the same year, [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] was co-founded in Beijing by the American entrepreneur John Oyler and Xiaodong Wang, a Howard Hughes Medical Institute investigator at the University of Texas Southwestern and founding director of [[entity:nibs-beijing|NIBS Beijing]]. BeiGene’s defining bet was the American-Chinese hybrid model. Wang’s HHMI credential gave the company scientific legitimacy that pure Chinese biotechs lacked. Oyler’s American network let BeiGene raise from US venture firms early; the company would IPO on Nasdaq in February 2016, well before HKEX Chapter 18A made pre-revenue listings possible. And it built FDA-grade clinical operations inside China to run global trials at Chinese cost.[[cite:beigene-wikipedia]] [[entity:innovent|Innovent Biologics]] followed in Suzhou in 2011, deliberately licensing Adimab’s yeast-display platform (a leading Western fully-human antibody discovery technology) rather than building one in-house. [[entity:junshi|Junshi Biosciences]] (Shanghai, 2012) and [[entity:zailab|Zai Lab]] (Shanghai, 2014) emerged in the same wave.[[cite:innovent-biologics-wikipedia]][[cite:innovent-adimab-asianscientist]]

[[entity:hengrui|Hengrui Pharmaceuticals]], founded as a small generic-drug factory in 1970, had listed in Shanghai in 2000 and slowly transformed itself into one of the world’s most prolific clinical-trial sponsors.[[cite:hengrui-wikipedia]] The first generation of products from this cohort was largely “fast-follower” oncology, copying validated Western targets like [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s blockbuster Keytruda where the science was proven and the commercial upside was enormous. The strategy minimized scientific risk but maximized crowding. At peak, nearly a hundred Chinese companies were racing in the same checkpoint-inhibitor space, and by 2020 China had become the world’s most prolific source of new combination trials targeting [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s Keytruda pathway.[[cite:globaldata-china-pd1-trials-2020]]

The 12th Five-Year Plan (2011–2015) elevated biotechnology to the status of a strategic emerging industry. In 2013, [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics]] vertically integrated by acquiring Mountain View–based Complete Genomics for $118 million, gaining its own sequencing-instrument platform that would later spin off as [[entity:mgi-tech|MGI Tech]].[[cite:bgi-complete-genomics-asianscientist]]

title: Harmonizing with International Regulations and Markets
body:
In January 2015, Bi Jingquan was appointed director of the [[entity:nmpa|China Food and Drug Administration]]. He had been vice-chair of the [[entity:ndrc|National Development and Reform Commission]], China’s top macroeconomic planning body, which gave him rare access to cross-agency leverage.[[cite:bi-jingquan-wikipedia]] Bi inherited an agency in crisis--roughly 22,000 drug applications were backlogged, and a CFDA self-audit that year had found that some 80 percent of submitted clinical-trial data was fraudulent or incomplete. In August, the State Council issued document No. 44, “Opinions on the Reform of Review and Approval Process for Drugs and Medical Devices.” [[reform:priority-review|Priority review]] followed in November; clinical-trial application timelines collapsed toward sixty working days; foreign clinical-trial data could be accepted without redundant local trials; and the agency was redirected explicitly at fraudulent data. Median new-drug-application review time fell from 200 working days to ~130.[[cite:ropesgray-china-state-council-44]][[cite:wang-2019-new-era-drug-innovation-china]]

Before ICH membership, a Chinese clinical trial dossier wasn't directly usable by the FDA or EMA, and vice versa. Each agency demanded its own format, its own studies, its own definitions of safety and efficacy, meaning a drug developer aiming for global launch had to do most of the work twice. In June 2017, the [[reform:nmpa-rename|China Food and Drug Administration]] (renamed the [[entity:nmpa|National Medical Products Administration]] the following year in the government reorganization) was admitted as a regulatory member of the [[reform:ich-join|International Council for Harmonisation]], formally aligning Chinese drug regulation with American, European, and Japanese standards.[[cite:fda-cfda-ich-membership]][[cite:nmpa-2018-rename]] Such standardization laid the groundwork for the outlicensing boom that followed.

On 30 April 2018, the [[entity:hkex|Hong Kong Stock Exchange]] introduced [[reform:hk-18a|Chapter 18A]] to its Main Board Listing Rules, opening the exchange to pre-revenue biotechs.[[cite:charltons-chapter-18a]] The regime was explicitly designed to compete with the American Nasdaq for “China’s new generation of biotech companies”; annual listings under the rule would peak at twenty in 2021.[[cite:pharmaboardroom-hk-2021-record]] Combined with the [[reform:star-market|Shanghai sci-tech innovation board]] launched the following year, [[reform:hk-18a|Chapter 18A]] largely solved the valley-of-death financing problem (the gap between venture capital, which peaks around Phase I/II, and revenue, which arrives only after approval) that had plagued Chinese pre-revenue biotech.[[cite:xinhua-star-market-launch]]

In December 2017, [[entity:legend|Legend Biotech]] (a GenScript spinout whose investigator-initiated LEGEND-2 trial in Xi’an had reported a 100% response rate in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma at ASCO that year) signed an exclusive worldwide collaboration with [[entity:johnson-johnson|Janssen]] for its dual-VHH BCMA CAR-T cilta-cell. The upfront $350 million was, up until that point, the largest ever paid to a Chinese biotech for a single asset.[[cite:legend-janssen-collaboration-milestone]] 

Just weeks later, [[entity:junshi|Junshi’s]] [[entity:junshi|toripalimab]] became the first domestic checkpoint inhibitor approved in China;[[cite:frontiers-toripalimab-first-domestic-pd1]] [[entity:innovent|Innovent’s]] [[entity:innovent|sintilimab]], co-developed under a 2015 strategic alliance with [[entity:eli-lilly|Eli Lilly]] that gave Lilly ex-China rights.[[cite:innovent-lilly-tyvyt-press]]

title: Illegal CRISPR Babies
body:
China's debut on the global pharma stage was complemented by ethically ambiguous forays into the science of gene editing. In April 2015, Junjiu Huang at Sun Yat-sen University published the first report of CRISPR-mediated gene editing in human zygotes (non-viable tripronuclear embryos), an attempt to correct a β-globin mutation that causes thalassemia. The paper had been rejected by Nature and Science on ethical grounds before appearing in Protein & Cell,[[cite:liang-2015-crispr-tripronuclear]] and within weeks the International Summit on Human Gene Editing concluded that clinical use of germline editing would be “irresponsible.”[[cite:intl-summit-gene-editing-2015]] The following year, Chinese researchers conducted the first reported human trial using CRISPR-edited cells.[[cite:cyranoski-2016-first-crispr-human]]

On 25–26 November 2018, on the eve of the Second International Summit on Human Genome Editing in Hong Kong, the Shenzhen-based biophysicist [[reform:he-jiankui|He Jiankui]] announced the birth of twin girls whose CCR5 gene, the immune-cell receptor that HIV uses to enter T cells, had been edited at the single-cell stage to confer HIV resistance. Not only was the editing technically sloppy (the twins were genetic mosaics with off-target effects), the ethical-review documents were discovered to have been forged and the consent forms were inadequate.[[cite:wikipedia-he-jiankui-affair]]

The case closed in December 2019 with a three-year prison sentence for “illegal medical practice.”[[cite:statnews-he-jiankui-sentenced]] Beijing then pushed through a substantive legal overhaul: the [[reform:hgr-2019|2019 Regulations on Human Genetic Resources]], the 2020 Civil Code and [[reform:biosecurity-law|Biosafety Law]], and the 2020 eleventh amendment to the Criminal Law (which criminalized illegal human gene editing with up to seven years’ imprisonment) collectively reset bioethics governance.[[cite:covington-hgr-regulation]][[cite:china-org-civil-code-1009]][[cite:covington-biosecurity-law]][[cite:loc-criminal-law-amendment-11]]

Since leaving prison, He Jiankui has been offered a position at a state-sponsored medical academy in Shenzhen. He remains occasionally active on X, where he tallies his funding (currently at $25073 from 715 private donors) and invites his followers to nightclubs in Beijing. His current 10 year plan includes receiving the Nobel prize in 2029 and being canonized as a Roman Catholic saint in 2030.[[cite:nyt-he-jiankui-2026]]

title: The First FDA Approval
body:
In November 2019, [[entity:beigene|BeiGene’s]] kinase inhibitor [[entity:beigene|zanubrutinib]] (marketed as [[entity:beigene|Brukinsa]]) became the first cancer drug developed in China to clear the United States [[entity:fda|Food and Drug Administration]].[[cite:fiercepharma-brukinsa-fda-approval]] Other 2019 milestones ([[entity:henlius|Henlius’s]] [[entity:henlius|Hanlikang]] biosimilar in February,[[cite:henlius-hlx01-approval-press]] [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui’s]] [[entity:hengrui|camrelizumab]] checkpoint inhibitor in May,[[cite:camrelizumab-first-approval-springer]] [[entity:beigene|BeiGene’s]] [[entity:beigene|tislelizumab]] in December[[cite:beigene-tislelizumab-press-2019]]) confirmed that Chinese pharma was producing globally credible assets. The capstone came on 31 October, when [[entity:amgen|Amgen]] paid $2.7 billion for a 20.5% stake in [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] plus a 20-asset co-development pact, the first time a top-ten multinational bought into a Chinese biotech as a strategic anchor.[[cite:amgen-beigene-collab-2019]]

In 2018, [[reform:changsheng-scandal|Changchun Changsheng was fined roughly nine billion yuan for falsification in rabies-vaccine production]].[[cite:chinadaily-changsheng-fine]] In direct response, the [[reform:vaccine-admin-law|Vaccine Administration Law]] of 2019 imposed whole-process responsibility on producers across development, manufacture, distribution, and post-marketing.[[cite:nmpa-vaccine-administration-law]]

title: Losing the COVID Race
body:
Then came COVID-19. By February 2020, parallel vaccine-development tracks ran across at least five technology platforms: inactivated, recombinant subunit, adenovirus-vectored, attenuated influenza-vectored, and messenger-RNA. [[entity:sinovac|Sinovac’s]] [[entity:sinovac|CoronaVac]] and [[entity:sinopharm|Sinopharm’s]] [[entity:sinopharm|BBIBP-CorV]] would receive [[entity:who|World Health Organization]] Emergency Use Listing in 2021 and ship into dozens of low- and middle-income countries.[[cite:who-sinovac-eul]][[cite:who-sinopharm-eul]] 

The messenger-RNA story, however, was an embarrassment. The [[entity:walvax|Walvax]]-[[entity:abogen|Abogen]]-Academy of Military Medical Sciences candidate [[entity:walvax|ARCoV]] reached Phase III in Mexico and Indonesia by mid-2021 but never received broad authorization in time.[[cite:scmp-arcov-phase3-mexico-indonesia]] mRNA had been identified by Chinese planners as a strategic platform of the future, and failing to develop one ceded the most consequential biotechnology of the decade to American firms. The platform of the pandemic was developed and commercialized first by [[entity:pfizer|Pfizer]]-[[entity:biontech|BioNTech]] and [[entity:moderna|Moderna]].

Even so, 2020 became a record-breaking year for Chinese biotech financing.[[cite:nature-biotech-financing-2020]] Chinese healthcare startups raised roughly RMB 190 billion (~$28 billion), more than double the 2019 level. The combined market capitalisation of China-originated biotechs listed on Nasdaq, HKEX, and STAR rose from $3 billion in 2016 to $140 billion by year-end 2020, a 47-fold expansion driven as much by the opening of HKEX Chapter 18A (2018) and STAR Market (2019) as by valuations of the underlying companies. Hong Kong alone hosted six of the year’s largest biotech IPOs, including [[entity:remegen|RemeGen]] ($515M), [[entity:simcere|Simcere]] ($461M), and [[entity:everest|Everest Medicines]] ($451M). Most tellingly, Chinese biotechs signed a record twelve major out-licensing deals with multinational pharmaceutical companies, carrying a median deal value above $900 million, clear validation that Chinese innovation was producing globally competitive therapies.[[cite:mckinsey-china-biopharma-dawn-2021]]

title: Commoditization of the Fast-Followers
body:
By 2021 the fast-follower strategy that had built the innovator generation had become a trap. China runs a single-payer healthcare system. The [[entity:nhsa|National Healthcare Security Administration]] (created March 2018) negotiates drug prices nationwide, and any drug that wins a slot on the [[reform:nrdl-resume|National Reimbursement Drug List]] or in [[reform:vbp|volume-based procurement]] tenders is bought by every Chinese hospital at the negotiated price. The fast-follower wave had produced dozens of nearly-identical Chinese checkpoint inhibitors and ADCs competing for those slots, and NHSA could play them off each other. [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]], the largest Chinese pharma by market cap, saw its checkpoint inhibitor [[entity:hengrui|camrelizumab]] enter the NRDL at deep discounts;[[cite:eversana-pd1-nrdl-2020]] would-be blockbusters became commodity goods almost on contact. The regulator’s [[entity:cde|Center for Drug Evaluation]] acknowledged the shift in 2021 with formal [[reform:value-oriented-onc|Guidelines for Clinical Value-Oriented Oncology Drug Development]], conceding that fast-following [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s Keytruda was no longer a winning strategy.[[cite:pharmadj-cde-clinical-value-guidelines]]

China’s share of global biotech venture capital climbed from 3.5% in 2010 to 18.9% by 2020[[cite:itif-china-biotech-2024]][[cite:nsf-skti-24-2022]] before reversing sharply in 2021, driven by a global post-COVID valuation reset, US interest-rate hikes, and US-China tensions pushing foreign investors out.[[cite:stat-china-biotech-foreign-pullback-2024]] By 2024, Chinese biopharma primary-market financing had collapsed from a 2021 peak of RMB 88 billion (~$13.6 billion) to RMB 27.7 billion (~$3.8 billion).[[cite:biopharmaapac-china-h1-2025]] Companies that could no longer raise at home raised abroad through licensing instead. Two breakthrough deals signaled the pivot. In August 2021, [[entity:remegen|RemeGen]] (a Yantai-based ADC specialist) out-licensed disitamab vedotin, the first homegrown Chinese ADC, to [[entity:seagen|Seagen]] for $2.6 billion, then a record for Chinese oncology licensing.[[cite:businesswire-seagen-and-remegen-announce-exclusive-wo]] In December 2022, [[entity:merck-co|Merck]] expanded a $9 billion ADC pact with [[entity:kelun-bio|Kelun Biotech]], the oncology arm of Sichuan Kelun Pharmaceutical.[[cite:merck-kelun-adc-deal-press]]

title: Becoming a Global Supplier
body:
[[entity:fosun-kite|Fosun Kite]] (a Shanghai joint venture between Fosun Pharma and Kite/Gilead) began local manufacture of axicabtagene ciloleucel (Yescarta) in June 2021.[[cite:kite-fosun-first-cart-china]] [[entity:jw-therapeutics|JW Therapeutics]], a Shanghai cell-therapy biotech, followed in September with [[entity:jw-therapeutics|relmacabtagene autoleucel]], the first domestic engineered T-cell therapy approved.[[cite:jw-relma-cel-press]] On 28 February 2022 the [[entity:fda|FDA]] approved [[entity:legend|Legend Biotech]]’s [[entity:legend|Carvykti]] (ciltacabtagene autoleucel), partnered with [[entity:johnson-johnson|Janssen]]. Carvykti was the first Chinese-discovered cell therapy to clear the American market, and the first Chinese drug to compete on a novel construct (a dual-VHH BCMA design) rather than as a fast-follower; it validated the LEGEND-2 dataset that had triggered the $350M Janssen upfront five years earlier.[[cite:fda-carvykti-approval-2022]][[cite:fiercepharma-jj-legend-carvykti-fda-2022]] By 2022, Chinese companies were running 1.5 such programs for every one in American development. As recently as 2014, the United States had developed or co-developed more than half of all newly identified candidates in the class.[[cite:pharma-tech-cart-china-superpower]]

By 2024, China was no longer a downstream consumer of Western drug innovation but an upstream supplier of it. Chinese biopharma licensing deals more than doubled, surging from $16.6 billion in 2023 to $41.5 billion in 2024, with Chinese assets accounting for roughly 28% of all in-licensed innovator drugs at large pharma globally. Antibody-drug-conjugate licensing alone reached $10 billion.[[cite:globaldata-china-licensing-2024]] [[entity:hansoh|Hansoh Pharmaceutical]], China’s third-largest pharma by market cap, signed two [[entity:gsk|GSK]] ADC deals in late 2023 each above $1.5 billion in headline value,[[cite:gsk-hansoh-hs20089]][[cite:gsk-hansoh-hs20093]] then closed a $2 billion [[entity:merck-co|Merck]] oral-GLP-1 deal a year later.[[cite:biospace-merck-hansoh-2b-glp1]] Upfront payments from China’s 2024 out-licensings (around $3.1 billion in October alone) for the first time exceeded primary-market biotech listing proceeds in China,[[cite:biopharmaapac-china-h1-2025]][[cite:scmp-china-out-licensing-2025]] making out-licensing the primary capital channel.[[cite:nature-china-licensing-2025]]

In September 2024, [[entity:akeso|Akeso’s]] Phase III HARMONi-2 trial of [[entity:akeso|ivonescimab]], a bispecific antibody targeting both an immune checkpoint and a tumour-vasculature growth factor, reported a forty-nine percent reduction in the risk of disease progression or death versus [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s Keytruda monotherapy.[[cite:akeso-harmoni2-wclc-2024]][[cite:iaslc-harmoni2-2024]] Summit Therapeutics had licensed the molecule in 2022 for $500 million upfront and up to $5 billion in milestones.[[cite:summit-akeso-2022]] It was the first time a Chinese-discovered molecule had decisively beaten [[entity:merck-co|Merck]]’s thirty-billion-dollar-a-year flagship in a head-to-head trial. Two months later, [[entity:merck-co|Merck]] hedged its position by signing a $3.3-billion deal for [[entity:lanova|LaNova’s]] LM-299, a same-class PD-1/VEGF bispecific in earlier development.[[cite:merck-lanova-2024]]

In 2025 the licensing wave hit its peak. China’s outbound deals reached 157 transactions worth roughly $135.7 billion (more than double 2024),[[cite:scmp-china-out-licensing-2025]] with American drugmakers signing fourteen China-asset deals worth $18.3 billion in the first half alone.[[cite:gabi-h1-2025-china-deals]] Roughly a third of all big-pharma licensing spending in 2025 sourced from China.[[cite:biopharmadive-china-licensing-tracker-2025]] The largest single-asset transaction was [[entity:3sbio|3SBio]]’s May 2025 license-out of its in-house PD-1/VEGF bispecific SSGJ-707 to [[entity:pfizer|Pfizer]], worth $1.25 billion upfront and up to $6 billion all-in, the largest single-asset out-licensing by a Chinese biotech to that point.[[cite:fiercebiotech-pfizer-pays-3sbio-1-25b-for-pd-1xvegf-bi]][[cite:pfizer-pfizer-completes-licensing-agreement-wit]]

title: The US Becomes Scared of CCP Spies
body:
The [[reform:biosecure|BIOSECURE Act]] passed the [[entity:us-congress|United States House of Representatives]] 306 to 81 in September 2024[[cite:ropesgray-biosecure-house-2024]][[cite:mwc-biosecure-house-2024]] and was [[reform:biosecure-enacted|signed into law]] on 17 December 2025 as Section 851 of the fiscal-year 2026 National Defense Authorization Act.[[cite:lw-biosecure-becomes-law-2025]][[cite:arnold-porter-biosecure-law-2025]] The bill was driven by U.S. national-security concerns about Chinese biopharma access to American patient genetic data and the [[entity:bgi|Beijing Genomics Institute]]’s documented links to the People’s Liberation Army. It restricted American executive-agency procurement and federal grants from “biotechnology companies of concern,” automatically capturing BGI and three of its affiliates through the Pentagon’s 1260H list of Chinese military companies.[[cite:foley-biosecure-2024]]

[[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi]] divested its American medical-device testing operations and its Oxford Genetics and WuXi Advanced Therapies cell-and-gene-therapy units.[[cite:wuxi-namsa-2025]][[cite:wuxi-altaris-atu-2024]][[cite:yicai-wuxi-altaris-2024]] [[entity:beigene|BeiGene]] rebranded as [[entity:beigene|BeOne Medicines]] and redomiciled from the Cayman Islands to Basel, signaling corporate independence from any single state.[[cite:beone-launch-bw-2025]][[cite:beone-wikipedia]] The canonical American-Chinese hybrid biotech was actively distancing itself from China as a corporate identity, even as it remained, by every operating metric, the same firm. American venture firms, which had backed many of the major Chinese biotech rounds at the 2021 peak, had largely withdrawn from China deals by 2025.[[cite:stat-china-biotech-foreign-pullback-2024]]

title: First AI-Discovered Drug
body:
In 2025, [[entity:insilico|Insilico Medicine]] published positive Phase II data for [[entity:insilico|rentosertib]], a treatment for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis whose target, molecule, and trial design were all chosen and built by AI rather than by human researchers. It was the first AI-discovered drug to clear a Phase II efficacy bar in humans.[[cite:insilico-rentosertib-press-2025]][[cite:nature-medicine-tnik-rentosertib-2025]] They IPOed on the HKSE in December of 2025 at $2.28 billion with a $290 million raise. They have 30 programs total, 8 of which are at the clinical stage.

[[entity:xtalpi|XtalPi]], a Chinese AI-drug-discovery firm that listed in Hong Kong under the new Chapter 18C track for specialist-technology companies, signed a research collaboration with DoveTree (founded by Harvard chemist Gregory Verdine) reportedly worth as much as $10 billion.[[cite:xtalpi-18c-listing-2024]][[cite:xtalpi-dovetree-final-2025]][[cite:xtalpi-dovetree-loi-2025]]

title: The Modern Landscape
body:
The structural cause of China's licensing dominance has crystallized. Between 2025 and 2030, top-selling Western drugs will face loss of exclusivity covering an estimated $200 billion to $230 billion in annual revenue[[cite:labiotech-patent-cliff-2025]]; recovering this value requires the industry to produce ~60-100 new blockbuster drugs (defined as $3B+ peak sales) within the next 5-7 years, approximately 2-3x the industry's historical pace. As an illustrative data point, [[entity:merck-co|Merck]] generated $29.5 billion in revenue, 56% of its total business, from Keytruda in 2024. Its key patents expire in 2028 with sales expected to start declining next year due to biosimilar competition. Replacing such revenue…

China became the buyer of last resort for novel mechanisms because the rest of the world stopped funding biotech in 2022 to 2024.[[cite:stat-china-biotech-foreign-pullback-2024]] As a result, the average upfront payment on a Western-Chinese licensing deal rose roughly 230% between 2022 and early 2026, from $52 million to $172 million,[[cite:fiercebiotech-china-230pct-2026]] and the most active acquirers were Bristol Myers Squibb, [[entity:roche|Roche]], and [[entity:merck-co|Merck]], with [[entity:pfizer|Pfizer]] and Gilead the largest dollar spenders. The Hang Seng Biotech Index ran roughly 60% in 2025 on the back of it.[[cite:hsi-biotech-factsheet-2025]]

The serious innovator tier had crystallized around [[entity:beigene|BeOne]] (Brukinsa hit $3.9 billion in FY2025[[cite:beone-q4-2025-earnings]]), [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]] (now China's largest pharma by market cap), and a second wave of [[entity:akeso|Akeso]], [[entity:innovent|Innovent]], [[entity:junshi|Junshi]], and [[entity:zailab|Zai Lab]]. Akeso was the science bellwether. Its PD-1/VEGF bispecific [[entity:akeso|ivonescimab]] had logged four positive Phase 3 readouts, including a head-to-head defeat of Keytruda monotherapy in PD-L1-positive NSCLC,[[cite:lancet-harmoni2-2025]] triggering a class rush of more than a dozen PD-1/VEGF bispecifics in development.[[cite:nrdd-pd1-vegf-bispecifics-2025]] ADCs had become the category of unambiguous Chinese leadership, anchored by [[entity:duality|DualityBio]], [[entity:remegen|RemeGen]], [[entity:hansoh|Hansoh]], and [[entity:kelun-bio|Kelun-Biotech]]; in the first half of 2025, ADCs accounted for 8 of the 24 Chinese license-out deals exceeding $1 billion, with antibody-based drugs taking 10 more.[[cite:biopharmaapac-china-h1-2025]] Cell therapy ran on [[entity:legend|Legend Biotech]]'s [[entity:johnson-johnson|Johnson & Johnson]] Carvykti partnership and [[entity:astrazeneca|AstraZeneca]]'s 2024 Gracell acquisition. Services and infrastructure ran on [[entity:wuxi-apptec|WuXi AppTec]] and [[entity:wuxi-bio|WuXi Biologics]] (among the world's largest mAb CDMOs[[cite:bioprocess-wuxi-bio-cdmo-2022]]), [[entity:bgi|BGI]] and [[entity:mgi-tech|MGI Tech]] in sequencing, and [[entity:insilico|Insilico Medicine]] and [[entity:xtalpi|XtalPi]] on the AI-native frontier.

The capability picture was lopsided in ways the headline numbers obscured. Antibody, bispecific, and ADC discovery and manufacturing were world-leading, with Akeso alone targeting roughly 160,000 liters of total biologics production capacity[[cite:akeso-rd-manufacturing]], comprehensive in-house from discovery through commercial supply. Clinical-trial enrollment ran two to three times faster than American counterparts for indications where Chinese patient populations were deepest, including nasopharyngeal carcinoma, EGFR-mutant NSCLC, hepatitis-B-driven hepatocellular carcinoma, and gastric cancer, with per-patient cost roughly 64% lower (around $25,000 versus $69,000 for Phase 3 immuno-oncology programs).[[cite:lek-china-trials-2019]] By 2024, China-headquartered companies were initiating 39% of all global oncology trials, up from roughly 5% a decade earlier.[[cite:iqvia-global-oncology-trends-2025]] CRDMO infrastructure ([[entity:wuxi-bio|WuXi Biologics]], [[entity:asymchem|Asymchem]], [[entity:pharmaron|Pharmaron]]) had become the physical substrate of global biopharma's small-molecule and biologics supply chain. The strength tapered, however, in functional-genomics dataset production. [[entity:bgi|BGI]] dominated sequencing throughput and pushed spatial transcriptomics through Stereo-seq, but the upstream perturbation-screen production that fed the next generation of biological foundation models had not migrated to China at the same rate.

Beyond the redomiciliation and divestiture moves of 2024 and 2025, the NewCo had emerged as a third adaptation. Chinese-originated discovery assets were spun out into newly chartered, Western-domiciled vehicles backed by US venture capital, leaving the Chinese discovery engine intact while routing ownership and trial sponsorship through holdcos in the US or Europe. [[entity:hengrui|Hengrui]]'s 2024 GLP-1 obesity spin-out into Kailera Therapeutics with Bain Capital and Atlas Venture sat alongside the earlier Aiolos-to-[[entity:gsk|GSK]] sale, where a Hengrui-discovered asthma asset had reached a $1.4 billion exit through a Western shell.[[cite:gsk-aiolos-press-2024]] The mechanism preserved the economics of Chinese clinical-asset arbitrage while satisfying procurement-side restrictions. Across all three responses, the licensing flow rose under [[reform:biosecure|BIOSECURE]] pressure rather than declining, because the underlying economic logic, that Chinese clinical assets filled the Western patent cliff at lower upfront cost, only intensified under a regime that compressed the alternatives.
